focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 5

He crossed out the same approximation for the fifth time, and it still wasn't any closer.

His watch said half-eleven. His wand was propped against the lowest shelf with a Lumos on it, throwing a hard white circle across the page while the rest of the sub-collection glowed faintly around him. Gavin had been on the floor of the innermost room since dinner, the manuscript across his knees and his parchment notes spread around him in a loose semicircle. The stone was cold through his robes — it was always cold in the sub-collection, a deep mineral cold that the castle's heating charms didn't reach, or had never been asked to reach, as though the rock down here predated the agreements that kept the rest of Hogwarts warm. Beyond the circle the walls gave off their own faint glow, the self-lit stone that needed no torches and predated the castle's torch system by centuries, maybe longer. In the overlap between the two kinds of light his parchment looked yellowed, wrong-coloured, like he was working under two different suns.

He'd been here since dinner, which he'd eaten too quickly and couldn't remember. The walk back to Hufflepuff would take at least fifteen minutes if the seventh-floor corridors cooperated, longer if they shifted — they'd sent him on a twenty-five-minute detour three nights ago, routing him past the Astronomy corridor and back around through a stretch of hallway he'd never seen. He had Herbology first thing tomorrow. Professor Sprout had set them a practical on Fanged Geraniums and he hadn't revised.

None of which mattered because the passage he was trying to decode had changed character in a way he hadn't seen before, and he couldn't make himself leave it alone.

The notation in this section was denser than anything in the previous pages. The...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...the point did not aim at the isolated mark itself but at the empty space just beside it, as if the absence mattered as much as the mark. His tiredness dropped away so fast it almost felt like relief. He pulled every page that carried the isolated mark, then every page with a narrowing three-mark cluster, then his notebook. By the time the arrangement loosened he had half the manuscript spread around him again and no more idea what he was looking at than when he'd started. He wrote down the page numbers anyway, turned to the next sheet, and kept going.

He woke to Owen's alarm at half six with his wand still lit against the bed curtains and Sheet 11 pressed into his cheek hard enough to leave a crease. By dinner he'd been late to Herbology, given Flitwick the wrong wand movement when called on, and forgotten his Potions essay altogether. When he came into the Great Hall, Miren and Duncan were already eating. There was space farther down the bench. The place beside Miren was taken up by Duncan's bag and a stack of parchment.

Miren looked up once as he set his plate down at the end of the table. Her gaze went to his face, lingered on the bruised dark under his eyes, and moved away.

"Pass the bread," she said to Duncan.

He did. They went on talking in low voices about a first-year who'd cursed his own quill to bite him. Gavin tore his roll apart and listened without joining in. For the past two weeks Miren had been asking if he'd slept, if he'd eaten, if he was even in the room with them. Tonight she didn't ask anything. She had started adjusting around him instead.

By then Gavin knew one thing, even if he knew nothing else: he wasn't going to get anywhere alone, and if he wanted help he'd have to risk letting someone else see one page. The only question was which one.
